title: Skills, Tasks and Technologies: Implications for Employment and Earnings

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: A college premium of 68 log points implies that earnings of the average college graduate in 2008 exceeded those of the average high school graduate by 97 percent (i.e., exp(0.68) − 1 ≈ 0.974).
the college premium stood at 68 points in 2008, a high water mark for the full sample period
the college premium in 2005 was at its highest level since 1915

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: fell by 12 log points for high school and some-college males, and by 20 log points for high school dropouts
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Real earnings of males with less than a four year college degree fell steeply between 1979 and 1992, by 12 log points for high school and some-college males, and by 20 log points for high school dropouts.

at 18 years of schooling, it lies 16 log points above the 1989 locus

the male median was essentially flat from 1980 to 1994
the male 10th percentile fell steeply (paralleling the trajectory of high school dropout wages)
the female 90/50 rises nearly continuously from the late 1970s forward

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: expands rapidly during the 1980s, plateaus through the mid-1990s, and then compresses modestly thereafter

the real value of which declined by 30 log points over the subsequent 9 years

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: 57.3 percent in 1979, 48.6 percent in 2007, 45.7 percent in 2009
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In 1979, the four middle skill occupations–sales, office and administrative workers, production workers, and operatives–accounted for 57.3 percent of employment. In 2007, this number was 48.6 percent, and in 2009, it was 45.7 percent.
The correlation between occupational growth rates in 1979-1989 and 1989-1999 is 0.53, and for the decades of 1989-1999 and 1999-2009, it is 0.74.

the correlation between occupational growth rates during 1999-2007 and 2007-2009–that is, prior to and during the current recession–is 0.76

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: largest declines in France and Austria (12 and 14 percentage points), smallest in Portugal (1 percentage point), unweighted average decline 8 percentage points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In all 16 countries depicted, middle-wage occupations decline as a share of employment. The largest declines occur in France and Austria (by 12 and 14 percentage points, respectively) and the smallest occurs in Portugal (1 percentage point). The unweighted average decline in middle-skill employment across countries is 8 percentage points.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: increased in 13 of 16 countries, average gain of 6 percentage points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In 13 of 16 countries, high-wage occupations increased their share of employment, with an average gain of 6 percentage points, while low-wage occupations grew as a share of employment in 11 of 16 countries.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: grew as a share of employment in 11 of 16 countries
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In 13 of 16 countries, high-wage occupations increased their share of employment, with an average gain of 6 percentage points, while low-wage occupations grew as a share of employment in 11 of 16 countries.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: mean gain in employment in low relative to middle wage occupations of 10 percentage points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In all 16 countries, low-wage occupations increased in size relative to middle-wage occupations, with a mean gain in employment in low relative to middle wage occupations of 10 percentage points.

Nordhaus (2007) estimates that the real cost of performing a standardized set of computational tasks–where cost is expressed in constant dollars or measured relative to the labor cost of performing the same calculations–fell by at least 1.7 trillion-fold between 1850 and 2006, with the bulk of this decline occurring in the last three decades.
Nordhaus estimates that between 1980 and 2006, the real cost of performing a standardized set of computations fell by 60 to 75 percent annually.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: observed rise of 12 points, model predicts rise of 25 log points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: While the observed college premium rose by 12 points between 1992 and 2008, the model predicts a rise of 25 log points.

Between 1940 and 1980, the fraction of young adults ages 25 to 34 who had completed a four-year college degree at the start of each decade increased three-fold among both sexes, from 5 percent and 7 percent among females and males, respectively, in 1940 to 20 percent and 27 percent, respectively, in 1980.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: high school or lower fell from 57 to 42 percent, at least four-year college degree rose from 20 to 28 percent
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: the fraction of employed males who had high school or lower education fell from 57 to 42 percent between 1979 and 2007, while the fraction with at least a four-year college degree rose from 20 to 28 percent

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: service occupations rose by 4.4 percentage points, professional/technical/managerial occupations rose by 5.3 percentage points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: the share of males employed in service occupations rose by 4.4 percentage points between 1979 and 2007 while the share in professional, technical and managerial occupations rose by 5.3 percentage points

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: 85 percent of decline offset by professional/managerial/technical occupations for females, 55 percent for males with 45 percent accruing to service occupations
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: For females, 85 percent of the decline in middle-skill jobs was offset by a rise in professional, managerial and technical occupations. For males, this share is 55 percent, with the remaining 45 percent accruing to service occupations.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: hovered at 41 to 43 percent during 1959-1979, fell in each of next three decades with net decline of 8 percentage points
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: After hovering at 41 to 43 percent of female employment during 1959 through 1979, the share of females employed in clerical, administrative support and sales occupations fell in each of the next three decades, with a net decline of 8 percentage points.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: declined from 23.2 to 17.2 percent between 1959 and 1989, then rose by 4.2 percentage points between 1989 and 2007 (25 percent increase)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Between 1959 and 1989, the share of females employed in service occupations declined from 23.2 to 17.2 percent. It then rebounded. Between 1989 and 2007, female employment in service occupations rose by 4.2 percentage points (25 percent)

notes: This is a theoretical and empirical paper on labor market trends, not an experimental study. It presents a task-based framework for understanding how technology, skills, and tasks interact to shape employment and earnings distributions. The paper documents major labor market trends (1963-2008) including wage polarization, job polarization, and declining real wages for low-skill workers, then proposes a Ricardian task-based model as an alternative to the canonical two-skill model. The empirical analysis is largely descriptive and uses Census, CPS, and other administrative data sources rather than experimental designs. No randomized experiments or quasi-experimental comparisons of treatment vs. control groups are reported. The paper includes extensive caveats about data quality issues including earnings non-response rates in the CPS ORG (rising from 15.3% in 1993 to 31% by 2001), measurement error in wage data, and challenges in developing consistent occupational classifications across the full 1959-2007 period. The authors note that measures of job tasks and offshorability are "highly imperfect" and that conclusions should be viewed as "provisional."
